description: Python baseline: no shell=True subprocess, no unsafe YAML/pickle deserialization, no f-string SQL. The f-string-SQL rule overlaps the engine's built-in sql-interpolation (the floor) but also fires when only the pack is vendored elsewhere. Extended with: py-requests-verify-disabled, py-ssl-unverified-context, py-flask-debug-true, py-weak-hash-md5-sha1, py-insecure-random-secret, py-django-debug-true, py-jinja-render-template-string.

rules.message: shell=True hands the command line to a shell — use an argument list with shell=False (also matches multi-line subprocess calls)

yaml.load without a known-safe Loader (SafeLoader/BaseLoader/FullLoader, keyword or positional) executes arbitrary tags — yaml.Loader/UnsafeLoader are NOT safe; use yaml.safe_load, Loader=yaml.SafeLoader, or yaml.load(data, yaml.SafeLoader)
pickle deserialization executes arbitrary code if the bytes are attacker-influenced — confirm the source is trusted or use a data-only format (JSON)
f-string with interpolation passed to .execute() — use bound parameters, never string-built SQL

rules.//: verify=False disables TLS certificate validation on a requests/httpx/urllib3/aiohttp call — the connection is then trivially MITM-able, credentials and payloads exposed. The pattern anchors to HTTP-library context to avoid false positives on unrelated `verify=False` parameters elsewhere in Python code: it matches (a) any line that names requests, httpx, urllib3, or aiohttp and contains `verify=False`, OR (b) `.verify = False` (the Session attribute form). Generic function signatures with `verify=False` as a parameter name do not match. HARD: this is a near-certain TLS vulnerability in HTTP client code.
rules.message: verify=False disables TLS certificate validation (requests/httpx/urllib3/aiohttp) — the connection is MITM-able. Use the default verification or pass a CA bundle path (verify='/path/to/ca.pem'); never disable it.

rules.//: ssl._create_unverified_context() and the _create_unverified_https_context monkeypatch both turn off certificate and hostname checks for every TLS connection that uses the resulting context — a process-wide MITM exposure. Use ssl.create_default_context() (verified) and supply a CA file if needed. HARD: no legitimate production use.
rules.message: ssl._create_unverified_context() / _create_unverified_https_context disables TLS certificate and hostname verification — use ssl.create_default_context() and load a CA bundle if you need a private root.

rules.//: Flask/Werkzeug debug=True enables the interactive debugger, whose console is a remote-code-execution surface if the app is reachable — the classic Werkzeug PIN bypass. Never ship debug=True; gate it behind an env flag for local dev only. The `[^)]*` reaches debug=True inside a multi-keyword .run(host=..., port=..., debug=True) call but stops at the closing paren. HARD: a reachable debugger is RCE.
rules.message: .run(debug=True) enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger — a remote-code-execution surface if the app is reachable. Remove it or gate it behind an env flag for local dev only (app.run(debug=os.environ.get('FLASK_DEBUG') == '1')).

rules.//: hashlib.md5() and hashlib.sha1() are broken for security use (collisions are practical) — fine for non-security checksums (cache keys, content addressing, ETags) but a finding for password hashing, signatures, or integrity. WARN because the non-security use is legitimate; confirm the use case and switch to hashlib.sha256/blake2b (or, for passwords, a KDF like argon2/bcrypt/scrypt). sha256/sha512/sha3 do NOT match.
rules.message: hashlib.md5/sha1 are broken for security use (practical collisions) — ok for non-security checksums but confirm the use case. Use hashlib.sha256/blake2b for integrity, or a KDF (argon2/bcrypt/scrypt) for passwords.

rules.//: The `random` module is a deterministic Mersenne-Twister PRNG — its output is predictable from a few samples, so it must never produce tokens, secrets, passwords, OTPs, nonces, salts, api keys, or session ids. The pattern requires a security-NAMED variable on the left of `=` AND a random.<method>( call on the right of the SAME assignment (the `[^#\n]*` stays on the line and skips trailing comments). Bare `random.random()` assigned to a non-security var does NOT match — deliberately, to keep FPs near zero. Case-insensitive so TOKEN/Token/token all match. Fix: use the `secrets` module (secrets.token_hex, secrets.choice, secrets.randbelow). WARN so a confirmed non-secret use can be annotated.
rules.message: security-sensitive value (token/secret/password/otp/nonce/salt/api_key/session) assigned from the predictable `random` module — use the `secrets` module instead (secrets.token_hex/token_urlsafe, secrets.choice, secrets.randbelow).

rules.//: DEBUG = True in a Django settings module leaks stack traces, settings, and SQL to any visitor on an unhandled error, and disables some security middleware — never the production value. WARN (not hard) because dev/local settings files legitimately set it; the `^\s*` anchor with the `m` flag matches the module-level assignment at the start of a line, so `if DEBUG = True` style nesting or `MY_DEBUG = True` do not match. `DEBUG = False` does not match. Fix: drive it from an env var (DEBUG = os.environ.get('DJANGO_DEBUG') == '1').
rules.message: Django DEBUG = True exposes stack traces, settings, and SQL on errors and weakens security middleware — never ship it. Drive it from an env var (DEBUG = os.environ.get('DJANGO_DEBUG') == '1') so production defaults to False.

rules.//: render_template_string() compiles its argument as a Jinja2 template at runtime; if any user-controlled data reaches that string it is server-side template injection (SSTI), which escalates to RCE via Jinja's object graph. Render a fixed template file with render_template() and pass user data as context variables, never concatenated into the template source. WARN because a fully-constant template string is safe — confirm no user input reaches the argument.
rules.message: render_template_string() compiles its argument as a Jinja2 template — if user input reaches it, that is server-side template injection (RCE). Use render_template() with a fixed template file and pass user data as context, never built into the template source.
